competitors.label: Prevent DOM-based cross-site scripting vulnerabilities with Trusted Types
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev is a strong practical reference for DOM-XSS sink control, Trusted Types, and enforcement at risky APIs like innerHTML and eval.
competitors.winReason: web.dev is stronger on deep Trusted Types rollout details, but our page is now better for the broader DOM-XSS interview question because it connects concrete browser-side triggers, safe sink defaults, and the mitigation stack in one compact answer.
theirStrengths: It gives much deeper implementation guidance for enforcing sink safety with CSP and Trusted Types.
theirGaps: It assumes more security maturity and is less compact for a fast interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: A support widget reads location.hash to restore UI state and also renders agent notes from an API.
followUpConfusion: Escaping is context-specific output encoding. Sanitization removes unsafe markup when you intentionally allow limited HTML. Trusted Types does something different: it makes risky sinks accept only approved objects, so raw strings cannot quietly reach innerHTML or similar APIs.

realWorldUseCases: Our page now frames DOM XSS through a more product-like support-widget and API-content scenario, not only through generic sink mechanics.
actionableExamples: web.dev still goes deeper on policy setup, reporting, and rollout details for Trusted Types itself.
followUpConfusion: Our page now explains the mitigation stack more directly by separating escaping, sanitization, and Trusted Types in one block.

actionableExamples: Add one CSP violation-reporting example so the page covers rollout and monitoring, not only safe sink usage.
